Transcrição Completa: How to Manage Closers - Secret Shopping Strategy
[00:00]
"Alright, so one thing that I've started doing is completely taking over sales teams. And that's because my clients are really bad at running their own sales teams. And in doing so, we're up to about 70-ish people now that we actively manage as closers."
[00:20]
"It's really interesting dealing with closers at scale especially, what I find is that almost all of them consistently lie about how things are going or what they got going for them. And I needed to find a way around that."
[00:35]
"As an example of that, let me just give you a few situations that you're likely coming across as well. So, when we check in with closers and we ask questions like, 'Hey, how's your pipeline going?' How's your response rate? How's your show rate?"
[00:50]
"If they bitch about any of those things, we have so many best practices that we give these guys that we simply know right off the bat upon them bitching that they're not following the best practices. And we don't accept anything less."
[01:05]
"And let me be very clear when I say this, okay? I and my sales managers that we staff onto these accounts give these closers every single thing they would need to be successful to have a high show rate to max out their close rate."
[01:20]
"We do consistent training with them. We give them best practice SOPs in real time. We audit what they have going on and try to help them improve those things. And simply put, closers from my experience fall in and out of actually doing these things consistently. And that's where the issue lays."
[01:40]
"We'll get consistency from an individual for typically sometimes like one to three weeks at a time and then, they fall out of the habit again and they need retrained on the same exact shit."
[01:52]
"It's very important to note that when dealing with salespeople, sales people sell you with their words."
[02:00]
"So, when I'll give you once again another example. I ask a closer, I'm like, 'Hey, how's it going? You've got like 150 leads scheduled this week with you and you had 200 Hydra leads that were assigned to you. How's your connection rate with the hydra leads?'"
[02:18]
"And if they say anything other than I'm connecting with 30% of them on the phone, then we know they're not following the best practices that I put in that SOP."
[02:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Best Practice SOP Document"
NÚMEROS: 19 páginas
VISUAL: Documento físico sendo mostrado para câmera, páginas densas com texto, formato de manual operacional detalhado
[02:30]
"Let me just give a visual for the recording. Just to be clear, I'll post this along with the recording so you guys all have it again. But this is a 19-page best practice SOP for maximizing show rate and connection rate."
[02:45]
"Each one of these are just ideas that they could do. And once again, when people do these things, they do not have low connection rates. They do not have low show rates. They have really high show rates. They have really high connection rates because once again, they're following every single thing that's inside of this."
[03:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Sales Training Course"
NÚMEROS: 54+ vídeos
VISUAL: Interface de plataforma de treinamento, biblioteca de vídeos organizados, sistema de tracking de progresso
[03:08]
"In addition to that, I have my sales training course that we put closers through. 54 plus videos. We actually started filming some new ones as well, just based on managing closing teams."
[03:20]
"In addition to that, there's entire objection handling videos that are built for typically the types of clients that we work with. We always put all of our closers through different sales training programs. We have them show up for those calls."
[03:35]
"And thankfully when it comes to my trainings, it's like I can also track their progress. So we know right off the bat if somebody's actually watched these things or not."
[03:45]
"Now here's the point I'm trying to make. They will lie to us. The closers will lie to us as the sales management all the time. All the time. And they'll always say things like, 'Oh no, I am following the best practices in that doc.' Or like, 'Oh no, I did watch the sales training and I am doing all those lessons.' when in reality they're obviously not."
[04:10]
"So salespeople from what I found will immediately reform their behavior when there is data that is undoubtedly true, inarguable, there is no way around what you show them, they will reform their behavior."
[04:25]
"So as an example of that, if they're constantly saying like, 'Oh, the lead quality is shit.' and you as the sales manager or the business owner as an example, pick up the phone, call those leads, and close them. Inarguable, it's clearly you. You suck. And that overcomes that mentality on the spot."
[04:45]
"Same thing when it comes to show rate. If we instead take over those specific leads that they're claiming aren't showing up or they are having a hard time connecting with and we can blow their statistics out of the water, that'll get them to improve."
[05:00]
"The issue is that takes more time than secret shopping the closers. We found that secret shopping the closers is much more efficient. Much easier to do, keeps them on their toes because they know that it's happening."
[05:15]
"And in addition to that, gives us the real data of the situation for what the closer is doing. So we can then train that specific closer on what they need to do to improve."
[05:25]
"So how do we do this? Three things. Number one, burner phones."
[05:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Total by Verizon Burner Phone"
NÚMEROS: $30 smartphone, $30/mês SIM card, 5GB 5G data
VISUAL: Smartphone básico sendo exibido, embalagem de SIM card prepago, comparação de custos mensais
[05:32]
"Big fan of any kind of burner phone. Doesn't matter where it comes from. I personally buy this one. It's called Total by Verizon. It's a little $30 smartphone, and you can buy a month-to-month SIM card with it."
[05:45]
"This month-to-month SIM card gives unlimited talk, text, and about 5 gigabytes of 5G data. And then it goes down to 2G. But all you really need is about five gigs for a few emails here and there or maybe like a downloadable video that a closer would ideally text you."
[06:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "TracFone Alternative"
NÚMEROS: Custo mais alto no telefone
VISUAL: Comparação lado a lado entre Total by Verizon e TracFone, diferenças de preço destacadas
[06:08]
"In addition to that, the OG of this whole space, it's called TracFone. TracFone is, I think, the OG burner phone for all industries. Long story short, just a little higher cost on the phones."
[06:20]
"What we'll do, just to be clear, is for each team that we work on, we'll have one burner phone per client. So, we might spend, as an example, like $300 just to acquire these phones. And then we'll spend about 30 bucks a month on each one of these devices."
[06:40]
"Now, the benefit of this specific plan that I like the most is we can just trash this number after one month, and we can just buy a new $30 card the next month. And that's then going to allow us to have a new phone number, which is then going to allow us to have a new thing that we can do to secret shop."
[07:00]
"Now, outside of the burner phone, it's important to point out we also are going to create a new email each time that we create a new phone number. And that new email is going to represent the person that's being secret shopped."
[07:15]
"So, we could also see how the marketing automation is doing. Now, we're typically responsible for the marketing automation just to point that out. We just want to make sure it's not going into spam."
[07:28]
"When we create a new email, we get an idea of where it goes into. And it gives us an opportunity to create emails with Gmail, to create emails with the primary social and promotions tab or the updates tab nowadays, depending on which form of Gmail you have."
[07:45]
"Gives us the ability to create like some weird Outlook email or like an AOL email or some shit like that. Same thing. See where it all goes. Most of the time we're creating just new Gmails on the fly, just to be rather transparent."
[08:00]
"Now, point being, with our burner phone, with our new email, and with our new phone number from our SIM card that we purchased for our burner phone, now we're going to create a fake name for ourselves."
[08:15]
"That's typically going to be when you create your emails, you determine what your name's going to be, and we're going to start filling out the different forms that we have online for our different clients that we're going to secret shop sales teams for."
[08:30]
"So, we're going to go in, we're going to fill out an application, and we're not going to do anything else. We're just going to fill out the application and see if anybody calls us, if anybody texts us."
[08:42]
"We're going to schedule calls. We're going to see does anybody text me or call me upon me scheduling the call? How long does it take? Do they call or text at all? Are they sending video texts like they're supposed to?"
[08:55]
"Did I get any kind of sales assets like testimonials, social proof, landing pages? Is the closer being fucking lazy?"
[09:05]
"A lot of the times what we'll find, especially after a closer on the account for a while, what they'll start to do is very efficient for them, but very selfish and it gets a very low response rate."
[09:18]
"They'll say things like, 'Hey, this is so-and-so with this business. Thank you for filling out our form online. Here's a link to my calendar so you can book a call.'"
[09:28]
"It's like, WE NEVER TALK about that. We never teach them that shit. We advise against doing that shit, but once again, they find their way into that type of behavior all the time. Just happens."
[09:40]
"So, we want to see that occur. So, once again, we have literal proof that we can then turn around and show to the closer and be like, 'Hey, you know how you were getting a terrible show rate? Well, we just wanted to justify to you why we're taking you off the inbound lead flow because of this.'"
[10:00]
"And then we'll retrain them. We're going to reform their behavior and we're going to adapt them to what they actually need to be doing to be successful and maximize their show rate, maximize their connection rate."
[10:15]
"We'll look at the email as well and see like is the closer actually doing any kind of manual outreach at all because that all helps tremendously increasing the show rate and increasing the connection rate and increasing a close rate as well."
[10:30]
"If we miss a call, so if we schedule a call, even if the closer responds initially, if we miss the call, what happens after we miss the call?"
[10:40]
"Do we get any phone calls at the call time asking us where we are? Do we get any texts like that? Do we get any emails like that? How many do we get, if any at all?"
[10:52]
"How long do they consistently follow up after that call has been missed? Is it 15 minutes, 30 minutes, an hour, hours later, if anything?"
[11:05]
"And surprisingly, once again, as good as you can train people, it means nothing if you can't actually go and see if they're doing it or not. That is why we want to always secret shop people."
[11:18]
"We never want to leave it to their words, ever. That is the number one lesson I've learned with managing closers. Never, ever, ever take a closer at their word ever on what they're experiencing."
[11:32]
"Unless their statistics are high. If they have phenomenal statistics, let it ride. There's no need to secret shop them unless you want to reverse engineer their process to teach other people they're doing good."
[11:45]
"If they have a high show rate, if they have a great connection rate on inbound leads, if they have a great connection rate to scheduled call rate, they have a great close rate, let it be. Let it be. They're good."
[12:00]
"If their statistics are shit, then that's where you need to secret shop those individuals."
[12:08]
"Now, it's very important to point out and I want to make this clear. You have to obviously track statistics to be able to know which closers are good and which closers are bad and which closers need Secret Shop and which closers can be left alone or used to reverse engineer and train the others."
[12:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "CRM Incentive System"
NÚMEROS: Sistema de 4 níveis de priorização
VISUAL: Pirâmide hierárquica mostrando níveis de incentivo, sistema de tracking de performance
[12:28]
"Obviously that requires us to either have CRM incentives is what we call it where they're actually logging what they're doing and how it's working for them."
[12:38]
"So, we create incentive tiers where once again if a closer is not actively updating their CRM for who shows up, who doesn't show up, obviously who they close, things like that, moving people throughout the different pipeline stages."
[12:55]
"Our incentive system is deprioritization or prioritization in lead flow. We have scheduled calls and being prioritized on the calendar. That's at the very top of the incentive system."
[13:08]
"Congratulations, closer. You're doing the best. We're going to prioritize you above all the other closers. We're going to fill your calendar first. That's the tippy top of the pyramid here."
[13:20]
"Below that is just being on the scheduler, period. Not necessarily being prioritized, but just being on it."
[13:28]
"I want to be fair in saying if you have a big team of closers, like I'd say four to five plus, there's also like a mid tier between those two, which would be you might not be the number one prioritization, but you might be like the second or third person who's being prioritized."
[13:45]
"You don't want to be that fifth or fourth person because you're not going to get a lot of calls. You're going to get a handful."
[13:52]
"Below that, we have inbound lead flow, which is typically the hydra for us. It's an ad strategy for all those who don't know that I've created. It's just essentially lead forms."
[14:05]
"So, that could be opt-ins, that could be lead form fill outs, that could be applications that didn't actually book a call."
[14:15]
"Once again, prioritizing the calendar. Prioritize somewhat on the calendar, but not at the highest on the scheduler. Not on the scheduler, but assigned to inbound leads."
[14:28]
"And then below that is, look, you fucked up and you've had terrible statistics on the inbound lead flow. Therefore, we're going to remove you completely off of inbound lead flow because we don't want to spend money on a shitty closer."
[14:42]
"And we're going to keep you assigned to your current leads, allow you to work them to the point where some get closed, and then in doing so, we'll work you back up in the prioritization model."
[14:55]
"And depending on how much revenue you're able to pull, you might even be able to skip a few steps there and go straight back up to the top."
[15:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Weekly Performance Tracking"
NÚMEROS: 52 semanas por ano, revisões semanais
VISUAL: Sistema de tracking semanal, gráfico de performance ao longo do ano, métricas de avaliação contínua
[15:08]
"We do this on a week-over-week basis for the prioritization system. So, we are essentially keeping statistics on the closers on a week-over-week basis over the course of an entire year. 52 opportunities to get prioritized or deprioritized or dropped completely off of inbound lead flow or added back on."
[15:30]
"Through tracking the statistics and through having that incentive system both with CRM usage and the prioritization system, we then see accurate statistics that we can then hold closers accountable to to once again give us insight on what we need to do in terms of secret shopping."
[15:50]
"So if and when we need to secret shop somebody, it's super simple. We're going to try to get assigned to that closer as a lead through the round robin. So, we might fill it out a few times."
[16:05]
"We might fill out the lead form and then just assign it to who we want to on the back end. There's a lot of simple ways. Once again, when you're coming through a round robin to get assigned to the right closer that you want."
[16:18]
"Depending on the calendar system that you're using as well, you might just be able to go straight to the closer's calendar. Because as an example with Calendly every closer has a unique link that can be used as well outside of just the round robin."
[16:35]
"And we sometimes even just manually create everything and do the same process of notifying the closer through the marketing automation without necessarily having to fill out the forms."
[16:48]
"As long as it comes through and looks totally normal and isn't obvious to the closer that it's something different than a normal lead. That's all we're really looking for."
[17:00]
"And then as I mentioned all we're trying to do to be clear once we secret shop is we want to actually document data. So, we'll create usually just very simple notes in real time, typically handwritten. You can obviously do it digitally as well. Really up to you."
[17:18]
"And with those burner phones, we're keeping them by our side, and we're trying to log how many texts am I getting? And feel free, of course, go back and forth with the closers once they actually reach out to you and gauge how they communicate."
[17:35]
"Are they following your best practices that you encourage them to follow? Are they doing what you want them to do?"
[17:42]
"If they call you, you'd actually be quite amazed how simple it is to talk to a closer even though you probably talk to them routinely and have them not recognize who you are."
[17:55]
"Depending on the type of phone you buy, some phones make you sound like real shit. In addition to that, there's also little apps you could download on Android that allow you to change your voice when you're actually talking to somebody on the phone. We find those beneficial as well."
[18:15]
"Lastly, if any of you have spouses or partners or just siblings or somebody in your home that you can essentially hand the phone to and they can have the conversation, that also works."
[18:28]
"I have my fiancé secret shop all the time when I'm sitting there logging notes. I actually find it beneficial to have somebody else talk. It's a different kind of perspective that I get when I hear them on a conversation in comparison when I'm talking myself and trying to take notes at the same time."
[18:48]
"Point being remember we're trying to log the data both positive and negative so we can take it back to the closer and we can reform their behavior."
[19:00]
"Now here's the best part of doing this. Once you actually implement it yes you're going to go to the closer on a one-to-one level but it's also very important that you call out the closer in front of the entire organization of other closers."
[19:18]
"You want to let all the other closers know that you secret shopped an individual and that this is the data that you got back and you want to go over the pros and cons of what occurred."
[19:30]
"You want to show like, look, this closer's statistics have been in the dumpster. Here's why. And then you want to showcase how shit their follow-up's been."
[19:42] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Public Accountability Strategy"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Conceito de exposição pública de performance, estratégia de shame/praise em grupo, dinâmica de equipe
[19:45]
"This does a lot. First of all, it shames the poorly performing closer, which naturally is going to increase the effort and results that closer puts in in order to get out of the position of being shamed."
[20:00]
"We find this is a good tactic from sports when a player performs poorly in front of the rest of the team."
[20:08] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Basketball Free Throw Analogy"
NÚMEROS: 1 jogador, time inteiro na linha de base
VISUAL: Cenário de basquete, pressão de equipe, consequências de falha (time todo corre), dinâmica de alto stress
[20:10]
"Like I remember playing basketball when I was in school and we had this one drill. It was at the end of practice and it was to simulate high pressure scenarios in games."
[20:22]
"Everybody would line up on the baseline. The entire team would line up on the baseline and the coach would pick one individual say you come shoot a free throw and if you miss everybody has to run. If you make it practice is over. Everybody gets to go home."
[20:40]
"And boy was it pressure. That one individual called out in front of everybody would have to make the shot. And if they did, they were praised by the team naturally. So, they ended practice."
[20:55]
"If they missed that shot, and I've seen some intense situations, where if they miss that shot, if it was a hard ass practice already and they missed that shot and everybody has to run, there's a chance that person could get their ass beat in the locker room."
[21:12]
"At the least, they're getting verbally barraged by the rest of the team. And everybody through doing that. I'll tell you what, man. Next time that kid gets caught up to shoot a free throw, there's a high probability they're going to make that shot."
[21:28]
"And that's the same thing in this case when it comes to the sales team. We want to name and shame the individuals, showcase the behavior that put them in the doghouse."
[21:40]
"We want to show why it's bad, why their statistics are poor. Once again, because through proof, that's where we see salespeople reform their behavior and change and adapt and improve."
[21:55]
"Nothing else improves it. Their words alone once again too easy to lie to you too easy to manipulate what's actually happening and that's what they all do of course."
[22:08]
"So moral of the story when somebody does something good it's the same thing we want to showcase that as well just like the free throw analogy that I gave you guys."
[22:18]
"We want to put on display hey this person's closing really well they have a really high show rate we secret shopped this person and here's how it worked here's why they're doing so good check out all these examples."
[22:32]
"Dude it's amazing the things you can do when you showcase positive things the rest of the team generally improves as well because they get an opportunity to reflect on their behavior."
[22:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "High Connection Rate Case Study"
NÚMEROS: 80%+ connection rate, dezenas de dólares mais barato em cost per qualified talk, horário de contato: 8-10 PM
VISUAL: Comparação de métricas entre closers, destaque para performance excepcional, análise de timing de ligações
[22:48]
"The other night we had a closer that we were secret shopping who had a really high connection rate. Like his connection rate with total lead flow was like in the 80s, which is very high on a cost per connected call."
[23:05]
"And their cost per qualified talk was the lowest out of everybody by like dozens of dollars. Point being, we were like, how the fuck is this person getting such a good connection rate?"
[23:18]
"And we filled out some of the lead forms and we were assigned to that specific closer and I didn't get any calls until the evening. I started getting texts and calls between like 8 and 10:00 p.m. from that closer."
[23:35]
"And we showcased that to the rest of the team. We were like, 'Hey, so this guy's getting a really good show rate, really good connection rate. This is the time of day that we actually found this closer was making these calls.'"
[23:50]
"And then the closer of course chimes in and is like, 'Yeah, that's the time that everybody responds back the most.' Like we're dealing with busy rich people. I thought it'd be best to reach out after they were done with their day."
[24:08]
"A lot of people when I try to call them during the day always just tell me they're busy, but at night that's when I have my best conversations."
[24:18]
"All the other closers on the spot started trying it out because we talked about that in that specific time frame. And all the other closers immediately started saying, 'Oh, dude, this is such a fucking great tip. Like I'm getting a fuckload of responses now.'"
[24:35]
"And long story short, it's like just little things like that help improve the rest of the organization outside of just positively congratulating the guy who's doing the best. It improves the statistics is my point."
[24:50]
"So, long story short, this also all helps to enable you to see clearly what you actually need to train your closers on."
[25:00]
"So, when we show up to the call, let's use the example, they do everything right. They send a video text, they send some sales assets, they send over social proof, you text them back and forth and you engage in conversation."
[25:18]
"And then it comes time for the call and you show up to the call and once again you happily go through everything with them. Obviously, you're typically not going to put on video and you're going to have a Zoom account for free linked to that email that shows the different name and all that."
[25:38]
"Same thing. There's also voice changing apps on that app bar now on the side that enable you to talk differently through Zoom."
[25:48]
"Point being, when we go up on that call and the closer also does really good on the call, we're happy to also encourage what happens after the call, what kind of follow-up do we get?"
[26:02]
"How long was the call on average? Do they handle objections well enough? And point being, it's like all of this allows us to see clearly what we need to do to train the closers."
[26:15]
"Do we need to improve their on call conversation styles? Do we need to improve their follow-up after the call? Do we need to improve their ability to get the people who didn't show up to reschedule again because they're not reaching out properly?"
[26:32]
"Do we need to just improve the show rate, the connection rate? Do we need to improve what they're saying?"
[26:40]
"And remember, because you actually see what they're saying, there's no lying about it. There is no like it's a completely different conversation."
[26:50]
"That's the number one thing I want to point out is when I've gone to closers or when sales managers that we work with go to closers and they just ask them like, 'Why are you struggling with this?'"
[27:02]
"It's like the most vague excuse-ridden shit ever versus when you just approach them and you're like, 'Hey, so in a very positive tone like I saw what you had going on. We secret shopped you. Here's the experience that our secret shopper had. We want to go through with you on how to improve all this so you can get your close rate higher.'"
[27:25]
"There's no excuses that are paired with it. There is no reason driven responses. It's just like, 'Yeah, help me improve, please.'"
[27:35]
"And it's really good because they know that somebody's going to follow up and actually see if they did it or not. So there's a much higher rate of them actually doing the shit you teach after you secret shop these guys."
[27:50]
"So anyway, point being secret shopping your closers is I'm going to say it without exaggeration is I think the most important element of getting them to actually do what you want them to do."
[28:05]
"I've never found a more effective way. Not through different training experiences, not through retreats or some weird shit like that. Not through consistent calls with the closers, not through group trainings, not through one-on-ones, not through SOPs, not through training libraries with video courses."
[28:25]
"Literally secret shopping them, seeing their actual behavior, addressing the actual behavior with recorded proof and helping them in those moments. Unbelievable the turnaround we've seen on individuals on these accounts we work with. Unbelievable."
[28:42]
"So anyway, I wanted to pass that all down to you guys."
[28:48]
"Nick asks, 'Why don't you use the same one for all clients?' Yeah. So Nick, for the phone numbers, you can use the same number for all your clients at once, but we also like to do this on a week-over-week basis."
[29:05]
"So, it's important to note that typically having about as an example, if there's four weeks in a month, having at least four different phone numbers or four different SIM cards that you can swap in and out essentially."
[29:20]
"Four different sets of emails like it's just it's happening at such a high frequency that one phone simply put would just be a fucking pain in the ass to have to swap the SIM card as frequently as we do."
[29:35]
"But yes, in fairness, yes, you technically could do this with one phone at a smaller scale for sure."
[29:42]
"So for each week you're getting a new SIM card basically."
[29:48]
"Not necessarily. No. So we'll just buy in bulk. I mean technically you could buy them week over week, but no, we just buy in bulk."
[29:58]
"So, I have five of these and then we have about maybe 10 to 15 of these active at a time and we'll just swap them in and out of these different phones."
[30:08]
"And does that automatically give you a unique number?"
[30:12]
"Yeah, different ones like already tagged with the..."
[30:18]
"Yeah, TracFone does it too. That's another company that you can do this with. Theirs is just a little more costly for the actual phones themselves. That's why I picked this Total by Verizon."
[30:32]
"Which one is better than the other?"
[30:35]
"I mean they're the exact same cost in terms of this part of it. It's just once again the phone was like a $20 difference. So we went with a cheaper phone."
[30:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Prepaid Phone Requirements"
NÚMEROS: Diferença de $20 entre modelos
VISUAL: Telefones prepagos e cartões SIM sendo mostrados, processo de troca de SIM card, sistema de rotação
[30:48]
"But yeah, this is essentially what you're looking for to be clear. You do not want the original SIM cards just to be direct. But you're looking for prepaid phones and prepaid SIM cards. That's what you're looking for."
[31:05]
"Can you send the link in the group?"
[31:08]
"Yeah, sure. With the prepaid phones and with the prepaid SIM cards. Those are... hang on one second. Let me grab these links."
[31:20]
"So, you can easily swap it in and out. With the prepaid SIMs, it's like you just pop out the side, pop in the SIM, and then you have a different phone number as soon as you pop in the new SIM."
[31:32]
"Typically takes like I don't know, a couple minutes to maybe 30 minutes most to sometimes update the phone with the new data."
[31:42]
"And you also have to be careful of which phones you buy. You need to make sure that the phone is paired with that specific prepaid SIM company."
[31:52]
"Even though this phone as an example is Total by Verizon. It's like I can't go buy a Verizon prepaid SIM card. I have to buy the total prepaid SIM card with it."
[32:05]
"I'll just buy the exact same ones. That's fine."
[32:10]
"Do when you do the Zoom like so when you're fake doing a Zoom call, how do you make it so that your fake name comes up? Do you have to log into a new Zoom call then go on your calendar?"
[32:25]
"What we do is remember we have a fake email linked to that fake name for each specific closer that we're secret shopping in that example."
[32:35]
"So, long story short, when you log out of Zoom and it prompts you to log in, you just log in with that fake email and that name automatically pulls into the Zoom."
[32:48]
"Like how you see Chris right here in the Zoom. It says Chris Walker because he doesn't have his video on and he doesn't have a photo linked to it. It just shows the name."
[33:00]
"So, that's what we never link a photo and we just log into the email, the fake email that we created on a free Zoom account and that's it."
[33:10]
"And to be clear, if you're paying for Zoom and I join into your Zoom meeting, that doesn't make it to where the Zoom has a time limit, just to be clear."
[33:22]
"It's only when you host the Zoom meeting that it does that. So, yeah, that works. That works really well. So, he's logged in and out of Zoom pretty much for the Zoom meeting stuff."
[33:35]
"Got it. And you're just like, 'Yeah, like I don't have a camera on my computer. Is it okay if we just do it without my video?'"
[33:45]
"You could literally make up whatever excuse you want for that part. I mean, that's like the easiest part of the thing."
[33:52]
"Like, hey, you could be like Luke right now sitting there shirtless in his condo over there. He's like, 'Hey, sorry, man. I don't have my shirt on. I don't want to have my video on.'"
[34:05]
"Does that make sense?"
[34:08]
"Cool."
[34:10]
"Yep. All right. Cool. Does anybody else have questions on this? Anybody?"
[34:18] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Secret Shopping: Most Effective Behavioral Reform Method"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Comparação de efetividade: secret shopping vs treinamentos, cursos, supervisão direta, one-on-ones
[34:20]
"So, I just want to wrap it up with the moral of the story then. Moral of the story, can't express this more direct. This is the most effective behavioral reform I've ever seen working with closing teams."
[34:35]
"It is the best by so much. It is the most effective. It's better than video courses. It's better than literal being handheld."
[34:48]
"I personally think it's better than having somebody on the call with them in real time. Like if this person was in person and you had a sales manager just looking over their shoulder all day watching what they were doing."
[35:05]
"I still think that the secret shopping method is better because you're seeing their unsupervised behavior for how it actually plays out without them actually thinking somebody's watching."
[35:18]
"And that's the best time to observe an individual. You see the true behavior."
[35:25]
"And as I mentioned, when you record and document the data, it enables you to have accurate real time like this is where this person's fucked up and what they need training on instead of doing a shotgun kind of training approach."
[35:42]
"And sometimes, it's the funniest part, the closers will approach us and be like, 'Hey, I need help with this' and then when we secret shop them, it's like they're doing that just fine, but it's like they really suck and are struggling in another area that they might not even be aware of that they're sucking at."
[36:02]
"So, as I mentioned there, I'm shocked personally how little I've ever heard anybody who does sales training for a living talk about this."
[36:15]
"This is unbelievably effective. It is the best by far. So after anybody implements it, please give me your feedback as well. Let me know if you experienced the same."
[36:28]
"I am super excited about how effective this has been. It feels like we finally figured it the fuck out with what we needed to do and what we needed to know to actually train these people for serious improvement week over week."
[36:45]
"And we've seen tremendous result from doing so. So anyway, have fun doing it. Let me know what it results in for you guys. I'm sure you guys all make more money in doing it. We sure have."
[37:00]
"Of course, you guys got more questions, you let me know. All right. All right. Talk soon. Happy Saturday."
📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO
NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:
70+ closers gerenciados ativamente
SOP de 19 páginas para maximizar show rate e connection rate
54+ vídeos de treinamento de vendas
Taxa de conexão ideal: 30% com leads Hydra
$300 investimento inicial em telefones burner
$30/mês por dispositivo (telefone + SIM card)
5GB de dados 5G incluídos
52 oportunidades anuais de priorização/despriorização (weekly basis)
80%+ connection rate do closer de melhor performance
Dezenas de dólares de diferença no cost per qualified talk
Horário ótimo de contato: 8-10 PM
4 semanas por mês = 4 números diferentes necessários
10-15 SIM cards ativos simultaneamente
Diferença de $20 entre modelos de telefone
ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:
Sistema de Secret Shopping:
Uso de burner phones (Total by Verizon ou TracFone)
Criação de emails falsos para cada teste
Novos números mensais via SIM cards prepagos
Documentação manual ou digital em tempo real
Uso de apps de mudança de voz
Participação de terceiros (cônjuge, parceiros) nas ligações
Secret shopping semanal dos closers com baixa performance
Sistema de Incentivos por Priorização (4 Níveis):
Topo: Priorização máxima no calendário (melhor performance)
Segundo nível: No calendário mas sem priorização máxima
Terceiro nível: Apenas inbound lead flow (Hydra)
Base: Removido do inbound, apenas leads existentes
Hydra Lead Strategy:
Lead forms
Opt-ins
Applications sem agendamento
Sistema de round robin para distribuição
Processo de Reforma Comportamental:
Name and shame público para baixa performance
Showcase público de alta performance
Análise de follow-up após calls perdidas
Tracking de video texts e sales assets
Monitoramento de manual outreach
Reverse engineering de processos bem-sucedidos
Best Practices Monitoring:
Show rate tracking
Connection rate tracking
Response rate tracking
Close rate tracking
CRM usage compliance
Follow-up timing (15min, 30min, 1hr+)
ESTRUTURA:
Hierarquia de Gestão:
Sales managers staffados em cada conta
Sistema de CRM obrigatório
Tracking semanal de estatísticas (52 semanas/ano)
Trainings consistentes em grupo
One-on-ones baseados em dados
SOPs detalhados (19 páginas)
Biblioteca de treinamento (54+ vídeos)
Objection handling específico por cliente
Metodologia de Treinamento:
Treinamentos em grupo regulares
One-on-ones baseados em secret shopping
Reverse engineering de top performers
Public accountability sessions
Analogia do free throw do basquete (pressão de equipe)
Ferramentas Necessárias:
Burner phones (Total by Verizon ou TracFone)
SIM cards prepagos mensais
Gmail/Outlook/AOL emails falsos
Zoom accounts gratuitos
Apps de mudança de voz (Android)
CRM com tracking capabilities
Calendly ou similar com links únicos
DADOS FINANCEIROS:
Investimento Inicial: ~$300 em telefones burner
Custo Mensal por Dispositivo: $30 (telefone + SIM)
Economia: Dezenas de dólares em cost per qualified talk com otimização
ROI: Melhoria significativa em close rates através de reforma comportamental
Diferença de Custo: $20 entre modelos de telefone (Total vs TracFone)
INSIGHTS CRÍTICOS:
Closers mentem consistentemente sobre sua performance
Consistência dura apenas 1-3 semanas sem monitoramento
Dados inarguáveis são o único método efetivo de reforma
Secret shopping > treinamentos, SOPs, one-on-ones, supervisão direta
Performance tracking é essencial para identificar quem precisa de secret shopping
Showcase público (positivo e negativo) melhora performance de toda equipe
Timing de contato (8-10 PM) pode aumentar dramaticamente connection rates
Closers com estatísticas altas devem ser deixados em paz
DURAÇÃO: 37 minutos FORMATO: Gravação de treinamento interno/call de equipe com Q&A
